Old {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}} | Founding = (tradition) {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}} morning |Main shrine = Sumikiri {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} pine roof |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{jalink|April 4th|Q2508}}・{{jalink|October 15th|Q2919}} |Shinto ritual = Daidai Kagura (April 4th/{{jalink|April 5th|Q2507|5th}})|Map = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Yamanashioka Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yamanashioka Shrine'' (Yamanashioka Shrine) is a {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in Shizume, Kasugai-cho, {{jalink|Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720}}{{jalink|Fuefuki City|Q849434}}. {{jalink|Yamanashioka Shrine}, which is compared to "Yamanashioka Shrine" in {{jalink|Shikiuchi}}. (Yamanashi City)|Q11469626|Yamanashioka Shrine}} also claims to be a Shikinai shrine. . Located on the north edge of the {{jalink|Kofu Basin|Q11577819}}, on the right bank of the Byodo River, a tributary of the {{jalink|Fuefuki River|Q11599751}}, the shrine grounds are {{jal ink|Ozokyoji Mountain|Q11438742}} It is located at the southeast foot of Mt. === Company name === Since early modern times, it has been called ``Nikko Gongen'', ``Yamanashi Gongen'', and ``Yamanashi Myojin'', but according to the ``Shrine Detail Book'' cited in the ``Investigation Report'', it began to be called ``Nikko Gongen'' in accordance with the ancient place name in the first year of the Keian era, by order of {{jalink|Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481}}. , in the first year of the {{jalink|Meiji period|Q3125096}} ({{jalink|1868|Q7717}}), the company was renamed to its current name, ``Yamanashioka Shrine.'' === Enshrined God === {{jalink|Enshrined Deities|Q11591100}} Three {{jalink|Hashira|Q4817}} are enshrined: {{jalink|Oyamazumi-kami}} (Oyamazumi-kami), {{jalink|Yakami-kami|Q644201|Takaoka-kami}} (Takaoka-kami-kami), and ``Wakeikazuchi-kami'' (Wakeikazuchinokami). According to the shrine's legend, three gods were enshrined from the beginning, but according to the ``{{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932}}'' only one was designated as the subject of national rituals. === History === [[File:Yamanashi-oka_shrine_Koriishi.jpg|thumb|170px|Koriishi (Photographed in June 2010)]] [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №2.jpg|thumb|170px|Kaguraden (Photographed in June 2010)]] It is thought that the origin of the mountain dates back to an old belief in Mt. Omuro as a divine object, and it is speculated that the mountain name ``Mt. Omuro'' means ``a mountain where divine spirits are imprisoned,'' similar to ``Momoroyama'' ({{jalink|Mt. Miwa|Q860627}} in {{jalink|Yamato Province|Q907495}}. Furthermore, in ``{{jalink|Bunka (era name)|Q1373146|Bunka}}'' 11th year ({{jalink|1814|Q6943}}) in ``{{jalink|Kokushi|Q11577871}}'' (hereinafter abbreviated as ``Kokushi''), there is a mention that Mt. Omuro once rang as a sign of abnormalities in the country.According to the shrine's legend, in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}, due to an epidemic of epidemics, three deities were enshrined on the peak of Mt. Nikko Takachiho (which is believed to be Mt. Omuro behind this shrine) by imperial order and served as the guardian of the nearby area. Q316672}}, when establishing the district boundaries, several {{jalink|pear||pear trees}} at the current shrine site at the foot of the mountain were cut down and relocated, and the enshrinement site was named ``Kaigane Yamanashioka.'' ``Shrine Detail Book'' cited in ``Investigation Report.'' "Japanese Gods". . Even today, there is a ``Koriishi'' in the precincts that is said to have been a marker stone for the county border, but the place of Shizumu is {{jalink|Kai Province|Q85807 of {{jalink|Reisei Province|Q860290|Reisei Province}} 6}}{{jalink|Yamanashi District|Q11469930}}{{jalink|Yamanashi-go}}, so according to this shrine tradition, it is the shrine where the name of the county at the time and the current name of the prefecture originated.The Kasugai area, located in the Yamanashi district of ancient Kai Province, is home to {{jalink|Late Kofun}} and {{jalink|immigrants} from the late {{jalink|Kofun period|Q459939}} to ancient times. |Q11561926}} Archaeological ruins such as {{jalink|Stacked stone mound|Q11597152}}, which is thought to be a tomb system, are distributed in Kai 4 districts (Yamanashi, {{jalink|Yashiro districts After the establishment of (Kai Province|Q11390569|Yashiro}}, {{jalink|Koma-gun||Koma}}, {{jalink|Tsuru-gun|Q11643756}}), the early Kokufu of Kai and the {{jalin of Yamanashi District] It is thought that it was the location of the k|gunge|Q108779163}}, and there are collections of {{jalink|Nara period|Q189178|Nara}} and {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} {{jalink|Teramoto Abandoned Temple|Q11457899}}, which is thought to be a fallen ruin or an ancient {{jalink|family temple|Q11547815}} of {{jalink|powerful family|Q84066}}. There are also {{jalink|Kanga|Q104539349|Government facilities}} such as ``Yamanashi Prefectural History'', Comprehensive History Volume 1, Primitive and Antiquity (2003), etc. for information on the aspects of ancient Kai Province and Yamanashi County. . In addition, there are 20 Shikinai shrines in Kai Province listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho", but about half of them, 9, were located in Yamanashi District. (Koshu City)||Kinbe Shrine}} and Yamanashi City's {{jalink|Kinbe Shrine (Yamanashi City)||Same name}} are Ronsha) and {{jalink|Monobe Shrine}} There are similar shrines in other countries such as "(Fuefuki City) || Mononobe Shrine}}" (Matsumoto Chinza, Isawa-cho, Fuefuki City), but this shrine is located within the ceremony "{{jalink|Kaina Shrine|Q11577877}}" (There are multiple Ronshas, and our shrine's set shrine, Azuma-ya-miya, is one of them. Along with other shrines such as ``Oimata Shrine'' (which also has multiple Ronsha shrines, such as {{jalink|Kubohachiman Shrine}} in Kita Chinza, Yamanashi City), it is a shrine unique to Kai Province that bears the name of a place in Kai Province (Masato Hara, ``Divinity Award and Local Society,'' Prefectural History Volume 1, Chapter 6, Section 3). . Furthermore, the place name ``Kou'' (Kofu, Kasugai-cho, Fuefuki City) that remains to the east of the enshrined place is considered to be the location of the early {{jalink|Kokufu|Q1629088}} of Kai Province, and is considered to be the ``Gods of Japan''. , {{jalink|Noin Hoshi}} wrote a poem saying, ``Yamanashinohana of Yamanashi-no-oka, which blooms on the Kai ridge and drags its feet'' (``{{jalink|Fukishu}}''), and it is believed that this is because the shrine was famous since ancient times due to its proximity to the Kokufu.``Investigation report''. .According to the company biography, {{jalink|Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|In the Sengoku period}}, the leader of the {{jalink|Kai Genji|Q11577904}} {{jalink|Takeda clan|Q1210114}} It is said that they sent messengers to visit the shrine every September, and it was compiled in {{jalink|Keio|Q776775}}4 ({{jalink|1868|Q7717}}). "{{jalink|Kaikoku Shrine and Temple Records}}From July 4th year of Keio to 1st year of Meiji ({{jalink|Egen changed to |Q11498853}} in September 4th Keio)" This is a compilation of the history and traditions of shrines and {{jalink|temples|Q5393308}} in Yamanashi Prefecture that were submitted to the head of the Kofuji Shrine. '' (hereinafter referred to as the ``Shaki'') also states that whenever the Takeda army went to battle, a shrine visit was held as the {{jalink|guardian deity|Q767655}} of {{jalink|Tsujigasaki-kan|Q11637200}}, and various offerings were made. However, at the end of the Sengoku period, around the beginning of the {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058|Tensho era}} (later 16th century), the clan declined, and over the shrine position, Hashidate Myojin Shrine (currently Hashidate Chinza, Ichinomiya-cho, Fuefuki City, {{jalink|Kaina Shrine #Kaina Shrine (Hashidate, Ichinomiya-cho, Fuefuki City)|Q11577877|Kaina Shrine}}) and {{jalink|dispute}} arose, and the Negi named Fujibei of this shrine was expelled from his position by the {{jalink|Negi}} of the Hashidate Myojin Shrine, and was placed under the umbrella of the same shrine. At that time, Hashidate Myojin had a powerful position that boasted its status as the ``parent deity of Kai Province'' (Kokushi), with shrine members from shrines east of the Fuefuki River gathering for its festivals (Kokushi), and it is thought that the shrine was intended to be under its control based on that authority; , after the {{jalink|Honnoji Incident|Q169598}} in the 10th year of Tensho ({{jalink|1582|Q6719}}), Kai Province fought between {{jalink|Ujinao Hojo|Q1156668}} and {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}}. When it came to the place of capture, the Hashidate Myojin {{jalink|shake|Q11588367|shake shu}} sided with the Hojo side, and when the Hojo army was defeated by the Tokugawa army, the shrine shu were also annihilated and their authority was lost, and in October of the same year, the Tokugawa clan returned the position of Negi-shoku to Tobei, breaking away from Hashidate Myojin's control. According to the "Shaki", various towns such as Shizume, Komatsu, Yamazaki, and Kokuga, which were designated as {{jalink|jinryo}} by Ieyasu on April 18, 1988, are also located in the Kasugai area (Komatsu, Kasugai-cho, Fuefuki City), Yamazaki is in the Isawa area (Yamazaki, Isawa-cho, same city), and Kokuga is in the Misaka area (Kokuga, Misaka-machi, same city). Total 4{{jalink|kan|Q116911247}}803{{jalink|text (Currency unit)|Q6279611|Text}} was {{jalink|relief|Q11450498}}, and at the same time, the divine territory of Azumaya-san Gongen (Azumaya Shrine), which was {{jalink|Setsusha|Q135009904}}, was also relieved as ``Shiasan territory.'' These were also confirmed and maintained in {{jalink|Ina Tadatsugu|Q3387913}}'s five country sou {{jalink|chiken|Q1092846}} in the same year, but Tadatsugu also relieved {{jalink|Kuroinji}} on November 23 of the same year (``Kokushi'').In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, in August of the first year of {{jalink|Keian|Q1146135}} ({{jalink|1648|Q6941}}), 8{{jalink|stone (Unit) | Q855048| Mountains were also exempted from various duties such as red stamp land and forestry bamboo trees, but these red seal land were followed by successive {{jalink|Seii Taishogun|Q131767|shogun}} thereafter. In the 5th year of Meiji ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}), it was listed as {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}}. === Shinto ritual === [[File:Yamananashi-oka shrine Daidai Kagura A.jpg|thumb|170px|Ama no Iwato no Mai from Daidai Kagura (Photographed on April 4, 2011)]] [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine Daidai Kagura B.JPG|left|thumb|170px|Ama no Iwato Dance from Daidai Kagura (Photographed April 4, 2011)]] There are festivals held in spring and autumn, and according to the "Shaki", in the past, {{jalink|portable shrines|Q979940}} were carried to Mt. Omuro, the former site of the shrine, on {{jalink|Hatsuuma|Q510847}} days in March and November. Currently, the annual festival is held on {{jalink|April 4th|Q2508}} and {{jalink|October 15th|Q2919}}.At the annual spring festival, {{jalink|Daida Kagura}} is performed for two days and nights (April 4th and 5th). It is also called ``Takeda Shingen's Departure Kagura,'' and according to the shrine's legend, it was dedicated to the Takeda army as a prayer for victory when they departed for battle. The dancers are called "Maiko" and were once performed by {{jalink|Shinto priests|Q60523}}, but later they were selected from among the {{jalink|parishioners|Q125562175|middle parishioners}} and have been passed down to the present day.{{j alink|hayashi|Q4171186}} is played by three types of instruments: {{jalink|taiko|Q11404}}, {{jalink|kengu|Q15614634}}, and {{jalink|flute|Q11405}}. Furthermore, the ``Shaki'' records the origin of this kagura in {{jalink|Enpo|Q1145735}} 3rd year ({{jalink|1675|Q7624}}), which is considered to be the time when offerings at spring festivals became established.``Encyclopedia of Japanese Festival Culture.'' . {{jalink|1967|Q2648}} ({{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}42) On August 7, it was designated as an {{jalink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property}} of Yamanashi Prefecture.It belongs to the so-called {{jalink|Izumo Kagura}} lineage, which expresses the world of {{jalink|Japanese mythology|Q814158|Kikiki mythology}}, centering on the story of {{jalink|Amano Iwato}}. There are 24 types of dances that have been passed down, and the order of number 24 is: 1. Four directions dance, 2. Funeral dance, 3. Gokansho dance, 4. Kunijotachi no Mikoto dance, 5. Yin-Yang dance, 6. Ukebochi no dance, 7. Four bow dance, 8. Dance of Tenden-onna no Mikoto, 9. Dance of Life of Sarutahiko, 10. Dance of Orochi, 11. Dance of Ama-no-Iwato, 12. Dance of Offerings, 13. Dance of Oratory, 14. Dance of Two Bows, 15. Dance of Kotosushiro no Mikoto, 16. Dance of Two Swords, 17. Five elements dance, 18. Dongyoku dance, 19. Naginata Ryoken dance, 20. Four swords dance (Kume mai), 21. Houken Kyozo dance, 22. Chihiki (Chihiki) dance, 23. Bow tengu dance, 24. Oyama Gimei dance. It is said that the program and structure (sequence) of the songs have not changed since ancient times, starting with dances to purify the place where kagura is dedicated, such as ``Shiho-mai'' and ``Female Hall Dance.'' At {{jalink|noon|Q168182}}, the centerpiece ``Ama no Iwato-no-Mai'' is dedicated, and finally, ``Oyama-Gi-no-Mikoto-no-Mai,'' in which the deity Oyama-Gumi appears, is supposed to be performed. Maiko is basically a male, but only the 8th song "Tencho Onna no Mikoto no Mai" and the 19th song "Naginata Ryoken no Mai" are girls with {{jalink|Facing|Q119379771|Facing (Hitamen)}} The dance and the final song, ``Oyamazumi no Mikoto'', is performed by the deity enshrined at this shrine (Oyamazumi no Kami), so it is decided that the chief maiko (Maikocho, the head of the maiko) will perform the dance. In addition, the 20th song, ``Four Swords Dance,'' is performed by four people using swords and bells as {{jalink|Forest|Q11498259}}, but it is also called ``Kume Mai'' because it is named after {{jalink|Kume Mai|Q55529628}}. === Kami no Kami === [[File:KINOKAMI Legendary creatures.JPG|left|thumb|100px|True image of KINOKAMI KINOKAMI. {{jalink|Kyowa|Q960763}}You can see the description of Spring New Year of the second year. (Photo taken on April 4, 2011)]] [[File:Shan Hai Jing Kui.jpg|right|thumb|140px|{{jalink|Shanhai Ching|Q844278}} "夔(Ki)"]] [[File:Ogyuh Sorai.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Ogyuh Sorai]] In front of the main shrine of Yamanashioka Shrine, a {{jalink|statue|Q11588751}} called ``Ki no Kami'' is enshrined. Cultural property (sculpture) designated by Fuefuki City. According to the 2nd year of the Keio era, ``Kinokami Raiyuki'' ({{jalink|Yamanashi Prefectural Museum|Q11469696}} collection), the age and provenance of the statue are unknown, but it is assumed that it was before {{jalink|Hoei|Q1145262}}3 years ({{jalink|1706|Q6835}}) based on the age of the recorded materials (described later).It is said to have been created by a craftsman from Hida, , and is approximately 66 cm tall. It is a strange statue of a lone-legged beast with {{jalink|tusks|Q185635}} and {{jalink|mane}} called ``{{jalink|夔|Q2987501}}'' that appears in the ancient Chinese geography book ``{{jalink|Shanhaijing|Q844278}}'' and is believed to be a talisman and ward off lightning. Although it is not normally possible to worship, the gates are opened once every 10 years, and now once every seven years.For more information on the Kōkami statue and the Kōkami belief, see Yoriko Maruo, ``God? Beast? The Descent of the Gods'' (Yamanashi Prefectural Museum, 2009). In the literature, it can be found in {{jalink|Kyōchu Kiko}} by Sorai Ogyu in the 3rd year of the Hoei era ({{jalink|1706|Q6835}}). Ogyu Sorai was a Confucian scholar who was a vassal of the {{jalink|Kofu Domain|Q661116}} lord and {{jalink|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Q846911}}.He traveled around Koshu on Yoshiyasu's orders, and when he visited Yamanashioka Shrine, even the priest did not know the origin of the statue. In addition, in 1886 (Q7822}}, a pastor and folk researcher who stayed in Kofu, "Kai It is also mentioned in ``The Fallen Leaves'', and Kyōko speculates that the statue, which was originally a {{jalink|guardian dog|Q20155846}}, was associated with the god of war and was worshiped as a protection against thunder.According to "Rayuki", in the {{jalink|Kansei|Q1195131|Kansei period}} (end of the 18th century), Teiichiro Yasuda, the accountant of the shogunate, visited this shrine on the occasion of river inspection. |Kyowa|Q960763}} In the 2nd year ({{jalink|1802|Q6883}}), the {{jalink|Tokugawa Shogunate}} ordered him to present the True Image of the Goddess. At this time, several hundred true images of the gods were taken and presented to {{jalink|Ooku|Q1064983}}, {{jalink|Gosanke|Q11071135}}, {{jalink|Hatamoto|Q572662}}, and others. Furthermore, in the late Edo period, interest in {{jalink|Yōkai|Q503186}} increased due to social unrest such as disasters and famine, and it is said that the belief in Kokugami spread among the general public. In addition, books of history have been disseminated, stories of miraculous experiences have also spread, and anecdotes have spread such as how the Takeda family brought disaster to the Oda army upon the downfall of the Takeda clan. It is estimated that the peak of the Kugami belief was around the Kyōwa period mentioned above, based on the state of wear of the Shinkage woodblocks, and in modern times hanging scrolls from the {{jalink|Taishō|Q329174|Taishō period}} can be seen, and divine cards are still being distributed today.Yamanashioka Shrine is a shrine that enshrines nature gods such as the {{jalink|Mountain God|Q839254}}, the god of water and rain, and the god of thunder, and it has been pointed out that the existence of folk beliefs in nature is behind the spread of the belief in the kami. In Yamanashi Prefecture, there is widespread belief in mountain gods, and {{jalink|rain praying|Q477738}} customs and {{jalink|thunder worship}} are also seen. Furthermore, in the {{jalink|Dosojin|Q269515}} faith that is common in Yamanashi Prefecture, there is a custom of decorating large grass sandals as part of the {{jalink|Michikiri|Q11641628}} event, so it is thought that the one-legged figure of the mountain god was commonly thought of. === Shrine building === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №3.jpg|thumb|170px|Main shrine (photographed in June 2010)]] [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №4.jpg|thumb|170px|Inside the shrine (taken in June 2010)]]{{jalink|Main shrine|Q2044994}} is said to be the work of {{jalink|Takumi of Hida}}. It is also said to have been built by a bansho|Q11578735}}, and although the details are unknown, it is estimated that it was built at the end of the {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}} based on its structural style. The roof is {{jalink|杮thatched|Q199388}}, and it is a one-ken shrine {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} with 1 front beam and 2 back beams, and 2 girders. It is a ``Sumiki-iri Kasuga-zukuri'' style in which a {{jalink|sumiki}} is placed on the pillar in front of the {{jalink|personal building}} (moya), and a {{jalink|kohai|Q11415970}} (kohai) is attached to the front. It is large in scale, and the characteristics of the time when it was built, aiming for new expressions, can be recognized in the overall variety of techniques and detailed designs such as the carving of a picture of a frog. . {{jalink|1907|Q2048}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}40) On August 28th, special It was designated as a protected building and was enacted in {{jalink|Cultural Properties Protection Law|Q1188502}} in {{jalink|1950|Q18597}} (Showa 25)It is designated as a national {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}. Furthermore, in the article dated September 7th and 8th of the first year of the {{jalink|Kyoroku|Q1193418}} year ({{jalink|1528|Q6415}}) of ``{{jalink|Odaiki}}'', there is a divine miracle that blood flowed from the {{jalink|rafter|Q1154705}}, but its relationship to the current main shrine is unknown.The {{jalink|haiden|Q3125745}} is {{jalink|irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} with 5 rows and 4 beams, {{jalink|irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} flat entrance. The roof is {{jalink|thatched roof}} (originally {{jalink|thatched roof|Q874531}}). Constructed in {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}15 ({{jalink|1702|Q6819}}), it has an atmosphere reminiscent of a Buddhist temple, and although it has not been designated as such, it is considered to be a valuable relic for understanding the prefectural early modern architectural style. Others include {{jalink|Kaguraden|Q94498}}. === Setsusha === ;Setsusha Azumaya Shrine:It is located halfway up Kitayama, about 1km north of Yamanashioka Shrine (hereinafter referred to as the headquarters). , enshrines {{jalink|Takeru Nihon}} and {{jalink|younger brother Tachibanahime no Mikoto}}. According to the shrine's legend, Nihon Takeru rested on a mountain road in this area during his {{jalink|Nihon Takeru#Eastern Conquest||Eastern Expedition}}, and the historic site was called ``Azumaya-san Gongen'' and was built to enshrine him. There is also a theory that it is compared to ``{{jalink|Kaina Shrine|Q11577877}}'' of Shikinai Shrine (``Shaki''), and there is also a theory that it is compared to ``{{jalink|Kaina Shrine|Q11577877}}''. . {{jalink|Tenmei|Q1194121|Tenmei years}} (1781 - "{{jalink|Koyo Essay}}" written by {{jalink|Tosei Kagami}} in 1789) records that it was once {{jalink|Sosha}} of Yamanashi District, and in {{jalink|Kaei|Q1146125}}4 ({{jalink|1851|Q7656}}), "{{jalink|Kaei|Q1146125}}" {jalink|Kai Sōki}}'' claims that {{jalink|Imperial envoy|Q11399920}} was sent when a major disaster or disaster occurred in Kai Province's {{jalink|Soja|Q1107129}}, but even if this is true, it seems that it was made a sessha of the head office at a fairly early stage.. Ieyasu's {{jalink|shuinjo|Q11093673}} dated 11th year of Tensho states that in addition to the head office, there were various towns such as Kokuga, Shizume, and Manriki.For Kokuga, see the note above, and Manriki is present-day Manriki, Yamanashi City. 1 kan 300 mon is relieved as ``{{jalink|Kai Province|Q858076|Koshu}} Shiasan territory.'' {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}8 ({{jalink|1603|Q6735}}) {{jalink|Four Tokugawa Magistrates|Q11490263}}{{jalink|Kuroinjo|Q 11678035}} also appears to be ``Shiazan Gongen,'' and a little more than 1 koku of divine land was donated ("Shaki"), but in September of the first year of the Keian period, more than 1 koku was made into a vermilion stamp land and was granted exemption from various duties such as forestry, bamboo, and wood. It was completely destroyed by fire in {{jalink|1989|Q2425}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} first year), but was rebuilt in {{jalink|1992|Q2060}} (1992). Furthermore, the {{jalink|cedar}} {{jalink|sacred tree|Q48743901}}, which had been designated as a {{jalink|natural monument|Q23790}} in Kasugai Town as a botanically valuable tree, was also destroyed in the fire (the tree was estimated to be 350 years old at the time of the burn).There are also two other shrines within the precincts of the head office: Tennogu Shrine and Shigeo Inari Shrine. === (Yamanashi City)|Q11469626|Yamanashioka Shrine}} also claims to be a Shikinai shrine. . Located on the north edge of the {{jalink|Kofu Basin|Q11577819}}, on the right bank of the Byodo River, a tributary of the {{jalink|Fuefuki River|Q11599751}}, the shrine grounds are {{jal ink|Ozokyoji Mountain|Q11438742}} It is located at the southeast foot of Mt. === Company name === Since early modern times, it has been called ``Nikko Gongen'', ``Yamanashi Gongen'', and ``Yamanashi Myojin'', but according to the ``Shrine Detail Book'' cited in the ``Investigation Report'', it began to be called ``Nikko Gongen'' in accordance with the ancient place name in the first year of the Keian era, by order of {{jalink|Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481}}. , in the first year of the {{jalink|Meiji period|Q3125096}} ({{jalink|1868|Q7717}}), the company was renamed to its current name, ``Yamanashioka Shrine.'' === Enshrined God === {{jalink|Enshrined Deities|Q11591100}} Three {{jalink|Hashira|Q4817}} are enshrined: {{jalink|Oyamazumi-kami}} (Oyamazumi-kami), {{jalink|Yakami-kami|Q644201|Takaoka-kami}} (Takaoka-kami-kami), and ``Wakeikazuchi-kami'' (Wakeikazuchinokami). According to the shrine's legend, three gods were enshrined from the beginning, but according to the ``{{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932}}'' only one was designated as the subject of national rituals. === History === [[File:Yamanashi-oka_shrine_Koriishi.jpg|thumb|170px|Koriishi (Photographed in June 2010)]] [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №2.jpg|thumb|170px|Kaguraden (Photographed in June 2010)]] It is thought that the origin of the mountain dates back to an old belief in Mt. Omuro as a divine object, and it is speculated that the mountain name ``Mt. Omuro'' means ``a mountain where divine spirits are imprisoned,'' similar to ``Momoroyama'' ({{jalink|Mt. Miwa|Q860627}} in {{jalink|Yamato Province|Q907495}}. Furthermore, in ``{{jalink|Bunka (era name)|Q1373146|Bunka}}'' 11th year ({{jalink|1814|Q6943}}) in ``{{jalink|Kokushi|Q11577871}}'' (hereinafter abbreviated as ``Kokushi''), there is a mention that Mt. Omuro once rang as a sign of abnormalities in the country.According to the shrine's legend, in the era of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}, due to an epidemic of epidemics, three deities were enshrined on the peak of Mt. Nikko Takachiho (which is believed to be Mt. Omuro behind this shrine) by imperial order and served as the guardian of the nearby area. Q316672}}, when establishing the district boundaries, several {{jalink|pear||pear trees}} at the current shrine site at the foot of the mountain were cut down and relocated, and the enshrinement site was named ``Kaigane Yamanashioka.'' ``Shrine Detail Book'' cited in ``Investigation Report.'' "Japanese Gods". . Even today, there is a ``Koriishi'' in the precincts that is said to have been a marker stone for the county border, but the place of Shizumu is {{jalink|Kai Province|Q85807 of {{jalink|Reisei Province|Q860290|Reisei Province}} 6}}{{jalink|Yamanashi District|Q11469930}}{{jalink|Yamanashi-go}}, so according to this shrine tradition, it is the shrine where the name of the county at the time and the current name of the prefecture originated.The Kasugai area, located in the Yamanashi district of ancient Kai Province, is home to {{jalink|Late Kofun}} and {{jalink|immigrants} from the late {{jalink|Kofun period|Q459939}} to ancient times. |Q11561926}} Archaeological ruins such as {{jalink|Stacked stone mound|Q11597152}}, which is thought to be a tomb system, are distributed in Kai 4 districts (Yamanashi, {{jalink|Yashiro districts After the establishment of (Kai Province|Q11390569|Yashiro}}, {{jalink|Koma-gun||Koma}}, {{jalink|Tsuru-gun|Q11643756}}), the early Kokufu of Kai and the {{jalin of Yamanashi District] It is thought that it was the location of the k|gunge|Q108779163}}, and there are collections of {{jalink|Nara period|Q189178|Nara}} and {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} {{jalink|Teramoto Abandoned Temple|Q11457899}}, which is thought to be a fallen ruin or an ancient {{jalink|family temple|Q11547815}} of {{jalink|powerful family|Q84066}}. There are also {{jalink|Kanga|Q104539349|Government facilities}} such as ``Yamanashi Prefectural History'', Comprehensive History Volume 1, Primitive and Antiquity (2003), etc. for information on the aspects of ancient Kai Province and Yamanashi County. . In addition, there are 20 Shikinai shrines in Kai Province listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho", but about half of them, 9, were located in Yamanashi District. (Koshu City)||Kinbe Shrine}} and Yamanashi City's {{jalink|Kinbe Shrine (Yamanashi City)||Same name}} are Ronsha) and {{jalink|Monobe Shrine}} There are similar shrines in other countries such as "(Fuefuki City) || Mononobe Shrine}}" (Matsumoto Chinza, Isawa-cho, Fuefuki City), but this shrine is located within the ceremony "{{jalink|Kaina Shrine|Q11577877}}" (There are multiple Ronshas, and our shrine's set shrine, Azuma-ya-miya, is one of them. Along with other shrines such as ``Oimata Shrine'' (which also has multiple Ronsha shrines, such as {{jalink|Kubohachiman Shrine}} in Kita Chinza, Yamanashi City), it is a shrine unique to Kai Province that bears the name of a place in Kai Province (Masato Hara, ``Divinity Award and Local Society,'' Prefectural History Volume 1, Chapter 6, Section 3). . Furthermore, the place name ``Kou'' (Kofu, Kasugai-cho, Fuefuki City) that remains to the east of the enshrined place is considered to be the location of the early {{jalink|Kokufu|Q1629088}} of Kai Province, and is considered to be the ``Gods of Japan''. , {{jalink|Noin Hoshi}} wrote a poem saying, ``Yamanashinohana of Yamanashi-no-oka, which blooms on the Kai ridge and drags its feet'' (``{{jalink|Fukishu}}''), and it is believed that this is because the shrine was famous since ancient times due to its proximity to the Kokufu.``Investigation report''. .According to the company biography, {{jalink|Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|In the Sengoku period}}, the leader of the {{jalink|Kai Genji|Q11577904}} {{jalink|Takeda clan|Q1210114}} It is said that they sent messengers to visit the shrine every September, and it was compiled in {{jalink|Keio|Q776775}}4 ({{jalink|1868|Q7717}}). "{{jalink|Kaikoku Shrine and Temple Records}}From July 4th year of Keio to 1st year of Meiji ({{jalink|Egen changed to |Q11498853}} in September 4th Keio)" This is a compilation of the history and traditions of shrines and {{jalink|temples|Q5393308}} in Yamanashi Prefecture that were submitted to the head of the Kofuji Shrine. '' (hereinafter referred to as the ``Shaki'') also states that whenever the Takeda army went to battle, a shrine visit was held as the {{jalink|guardian deity|Q767655}} of {{jalink|Tsujigasaki-kan|Q11637200}}, and various offerings were made. However, at the end of the Sengoku period, around the beginning of the {{jalink|Tensho|Q1145058|Tensho era}} (later 16th century), the clan declined, and over the shrine position, Hashidate Myojin Shrine (currently Hashidate Chinza, Ichinomiya-cho, Fuefuki City, {{jalink|Kaina Shrine #Kaina Shrine (Hashidate, Ichinomiya-cho, Fuefuki City)|Q11577877|Kaina Shrine}}) and {{jalink|dispute}} arose, and the Negi named Fujibei of this shrine was expelled from his position by the {{jalink|Negi}} of the Hashidate Myojin Shrine, and was placed under the umbrella of the same shrine. At that time, Hashidate Myojin had a powerful position that boasted its status as the ``parent deity of Kai Province'' (Kokushi), with shrine members from shrines east of the Fuefuki River gathering for its festivals (Kokushi), and it is thought that the shrine was intended to be under its control based on that authority; , after the {{jalink|Honnoji Incident|Q169598}} in the 10th year of Tensho ({{jalink|1582|Q6719}}), Kai Province fought between {{jalink|Ujinao Hojo|Q1156668}} and {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}}. When it came to the place of capture, the Hashidate Myojin {{jalink|shake|Q11588367|shake shu}} sided with the Hojo side, and when the Hojo army was defeated by the Tokugawa army, the shrine shu were also annihilated and their authority was lost, and in October of the same year, the Tokugawa clan returned the position of Negi-shoku to Tobei, breaking away from Hashidate Myojin's control. According to the "Shaki", various towns such as Shizume, Komatsu, Yamazaki, and Kokuga, which were designated as {{jalink|jinryo}} by Ieyasu on April 18, 1988, are also located in the Kasugai area (Komatsu, Kasugai-cho, Fuefuki City), Yamazaki is in the Isawa area (Yamazaki, Isawa-cho, same city), and Kokuga is in the Misaka area (Kokuga, Misaka-machi, same city). Total 4{{jalink|kan|Q116911247}}803{{jalink|text (Currency unit)|Q6279611|Text}} was {{jalink|relief|Q11450498}}, and at the same time, the divine territory of Azumaya-san Gongen (Azumaya Shrine), which was {{jalink|Setsusha|Q135009904}}, was also relieved as ``Shiasan territory.'' These were also confirmed and maintained in {{jalink|Ina Tadatsugu|Q3387913}}'s five country sou {{jalink|chiken|Q1092846}} in the same year, but Tadatsugu also relieved {{jalink|Kuroinji}} on November 23 of the same year (``Kokushi'').In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, in August of the first year of {{jalink|Keian|Q1146135}} ({{jalink|1648|Q6941}}), 8{{jalink|stone (Unit) | Q855048| Mountains were also exempted from various duties such as red stamp land and forestry bamboo trees, but these red seal land were followed by successive {{jalink|Seii Taishogun|Q131767|shogun}} thereafter. In the 5th year of Meiji ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}), it was listed as {{jalink|Gosha|Q134917316}}. === Shinto ritual === [[File:Yamananashi-oka shrine Daidai Kagura A.jpg|thumb|170px|Ama no Iwato no Mai from Daidai Kagura (Photographed on April 4, 2011)]] [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine Daidai Kagura B.JPG|left|thumb|170px|Ama no Iwato Dance from Daidai Kagura (Photographed April 4, 2011)]] There are festivals held in spring and autumn, and according to the "Shaki", in the past, {{jalink|portable shrines|Q979940}} were carried to Mt. Omuro, the former site of the shrine, on {{jalink|Hatsuuma|Q510847}} days in March and November. Currently, the annual festival is held on {{jalink|April 4th|Q2508}} and {{jalink|October 15th|Q2919}}.At the annual spring festival, {{jalink|Daida Kagura}} is performed for two days and nights (April 4th and 5th). It is also called ``Takeda Shingen's Departure Kagura,'' and according to the shrine's legend, it was dedicated to the Takeda army as a prayer for victory when they departed for battle. The dancers are called "Maiko" and were once performed by {{jalink|Shinto priests|Q60523}}, but later they were selected from among the {{jalink|parishioners|Q125562175|middle parishioners}} and have been passed down to the present day.{{j alink|hayashi|Q4171186}} is played by three types of instruments: {{jalink|taiko|Q11404}}, {{jalink|kengu|Q15614634}}, and {{jalink|flute|Q11405}}. Furthermore, the ``Shaki'' records the origin of this kagura in {{jalink|Enpo|Q1145735}} 3rd year ({{jalink|1675|Q7624}}), which is considered to be the time when offerings at spring festivals became established.``Encyclopedia of Japanese Festival Culture.'' . {{jalink|1967|Q2648}} ({{jalink|Showa|Q327567}}42) On August 7, it was designated as an {{jalink|Intangible Folk Cultural Property}} of Yamanashi Prefecture.It belongs to the so-called {{jalink|Izumo Kagura}} lineage, which expresses the world of {{jalink|Japanese mythology|Q814158|Kikiki mythology}}, centering on the story of {{jalink|Amano Iwato}}. There are 24 types of dances that have been passed down, and the order of number 24 is: 1. Four directions dance, 2. Funeral dance, 3. Gokansho dance, 4. Kunijotachi no Mikoto dance, 5. Yin-Yang dance, 6. Ukebochi no dance, 7. Four bow dance, 8. Dance of Tenden-onna no Mikoto, 9. Dance of Life of Sarutahiko, 10. Dance of Orochi, 11. Dance of Ama-no-Iwato, 12. Dance of Offerings, 13. Dance of Oratory, 14. Dance of Two Bows, 15. Dance of Kotosushiro no Mikoto, 16. Dance of Two Swords, 17. Five elements dance, 18. Dongyoku dance, 19. Naginata Ryoken dance, 20. Four swords dance (Kume mai), 21. Houken Kyozo dance, 22. Chihiki (Chihiki) dance, 23. Bow tengu dance, 24. Oyama Gimei dance. It is said that the program and structure (sequence) of the songs have not changed since ancient times, starting with dances to purify the place where kagura is dedicated, such as ``Shiho-mai'' and ``Female Hall Dance.'' At {{jalink|noon|Q168182}}, the centerpiece ``Ama no Iwato-no-Mai'' is dedicated, and finally, ``Oyama-Gi-no-Mikoto-no-Mai,'' in which the deity Oyama-Gumi appears, is supposed to be performed. Maiko is basically a male, but only the 8th song "Tencho Onna no Mikoto no Mai" and the 19th song "Naginata Ryoken no Mai" are girls with {{jalink|Facing|Q119379771|Facing (Hitamen)}} The dance and the final song, ``Oyamazumi no Mikoto'', is performed by the deity enshrined at this shrine (Oyamazumi no Kami), so it is decided that the chief maiko (Maikocho, the head of the maiko) will perform the dance. In addition, the 20th song, ``Four Swords Dance,'' is performed by four people using swords and bells as {{jalink|Forest|Q11498259}}, but it is also called ``Kume Mai'' because it is named after {{jalink|Kume Mai|Q55529628}}. === Kami no Kami === [[File:KINOKAMI Legendary creatures.JPG|left|thumb|100px|True image of KINOKAMI KINOKAMI. {{jalink|Kyowa|Q960763}}You can see the description of Spring New Year of the second year. (Photo taken on April 4, 2011)]] [[File:Shan Hai Jing Kui.jpg|right|thumb|140px|{{jalink|Shanhai Ching|Q844278}} "夔(Ki)"]] [[File:Ogyuh Sorai.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Ogyuh Sorai]] In front of the main shrine of Yamanashioka Shrine, a {{jalink|statue|Q11588751}} called ``Ki no Kami'' is enshrined. Cultural property (sculpture) designated by Fuefuki City. According to the 2nd year of the Keio era, ``Kinokami Raiyuki'' ({{jalink|Yamanashi Prefectural Museum|Q11469696}} collection), the age and provenance of the statue are unknown, but it is assumed that it was before {{jalink|Hoei|Q1145262}}3 years ({{jalink|1706|Q6835}}) based on the age of the recorded materials (described later).It is said to have been created by a craftsman from Hida, , and is approximately 66 cm tall. It is a strange statue of a lone-legged beast with {{jalink|tusks|Q185635}} and {{jalink|mane}} called ``{{jalink|夔|Q2987501}}'' that appears in the ancient Chinese geography book ``{{jalink|Shanhaijing|Q844278}}'' and is believed to be a talisman and ward off lightning. Although it is not normally possible to worship, the gates are opened once every 10 years, and now once every seven years.For more information on the Kōkami statue and the Kōkami belief, see Yoriko Maruo, ``God? Beast? The Descent of the Gods'' (Yamanashi Prefectural Museum, 2009). In the literature, it can be found in {{jalink|Kyōchu Kiko}} by Sorai Ogyu in the 3rd year of the Hoei era ({{jalink|1706|Q6835}}). Ogyu Sorai was a Confucian scholar who was a vassal of the {{jalink|Kofu Domain|Q661116}} lord and {{jalink|Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Q846911}}.He traveled around Koshu on Yoshiyasu's orders, and when he visited Yamanashioka Shrine, even the priest did not know the origin of the statue. In addition, in 1886 (Q7822}}, a pastor and folk researcher who stayed in Kofu, "Kai It is also mentioned in ``The Fallen Leaves'', and Kyōko speculates that the statue, which was originally a {{jalink|guardian dog|Q20155846}}, was associated with the god of war and was worshiped as a protection against thunder.According to "Rayuki", in the {{jalink|Kansei|Q1195131|Kansei period}} (end of the 18th century), Teiichiro Yasuda, the accountant of the shogunate, visited this shrine on the occasion of river inspection. |Kyowa|Q960763}} In the 2nd year ({{jalink|1802|Q6883}}), the {{jalink|Tokugawa Shogunate}} ordered him to present the True Image of the Goddess. At this time, several hundred true images of the gods were taken and presented to {{jalink|Ooku|Q1064983}}, {{jalink|Gosanke|Q11071135}}, {{jalink|Hatamoto|Q572662}}, and others. Furthermore, in the late Edo period, interest in {{jalink|Yōkai|Q503186}} increased due to social unrest such as disasters and famine, and it is said that the belief in Kokugami spread among the general public. In addition, books of history have been disseminated, stories of miraculous experiences have also spread, and anecdotes have spread such as how the Takeda family brought disaster to the Oda army upon the downfall of the Takeda clan. It is estimated that the peak of the Kugami belief was around the Kyōwa period mentioned above, based on the state of wear of the Shinkage woodblocks, and in modern times hanging scrolls from the {{jalink|Taishō|Q329174|Taishō period}} can be seen, and divine cards are still being distributed today.Yamanashioka Shrine is a shrine that enshrines nature gods such as the {{jalink|Mountain God|Q839254}}, the god of water and rain, and the god of thunder, and it has been pointed out that the existence of folk beliefs in nature is behind the spread of the belief in the kami. In Yamanashi Prefecture, there is widespread belief in mountain gods, and {{jalink|rain praying|Q477738}} customs and {{jalink|thunder worship}} are also seen. Furthermore, in the {{jalink|Dosojin|Q269515}} faith that is common in Yamanashi Prefecture, there is a custom of decorating large grass sandals as part of the {{jalink|Michikiri|Q11641628}} event, so it is thought that the one-legged figure of the mountain god was commonly thought of. === Shrine building === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №3.jpg|thumb|170px|Main shrine (photographed in June 2010)]] [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №4.jpg|thumb|170px|Inside the shrine (taken in June 2010)]]{{jalink|Main shrine|Q2044994}} is said to be the work of {{jalink|Takumi of Hida}}. It is also said to have been built by a bansho|Q11578735}}, and although the details are unknown, it is estimated that it was built at the end of the {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}} based on its structural style. The roof is {{jalink|杮thatched|Q199388}}, and it is a one-ken shrine {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} with 1 front beam and 2 back beams, and 2 girders. It is a ``Sumiki-iri Kasuga-zukuri'' style in which a {{jalink|sumiki}} is placed on the pillar in front of the {{jalink|personal building}} (moya), and a {{jalink|kohai|Q11415970}} (kohai) is attached to the front. It is large in scale, and the characteristics of the time when it was built, aiming for new expressions, can be recognized in the overall variety of techniques and detailed designs such as the carving of a picture of a frog. . {{jalink|1907|Q2048}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}40) On August 28th, special It was designated as a protected building and was enacted in {{jalink|Cultural Properties Protection Law|Q1188502}} in {{jalink|1950|Q18597}} (Showa 25)It is designated as a national {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}. Furthermore, in the article dated September 7th and 8th of the first year of the {{jalink|Kyoroku|Q1193418}} year ({{jalink|1528|Q6415}}) of ``{{jalink|Odaiki}}'', there is a divine miracle that blood flowed from the {{jalink|rafter|Q1154705}}, but its relationship to the current main shrine is unknown.The {{jalink|haiden|Q3125745}} is {{jalink|irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} with 5 rows and 4 beams, {{jalink|irimoya-zukuri|Q855025}} flat entrance. The roof is {{jalink|thatched roof}} (originally {{jalink|thatched roof|Q874531}}). Constructed in {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}15 ({{jalink|1702|Q6819}}), it has an atmosphere reminiscent of a Buddhist temple, and although it has not been designated as such, it is considered to be a valuable relic for understanding the prefectural early modern architectural style. Others include {{jalink|Kaguraden|Q94498}}. === Setsusha === ;Setsusha Azumaya Shrine:It is located halfway up Kitayama, about 1km north of Yamanashioka Shrine (hereinafter referred to as the headquarters). , enshrines {{jalink|Takeru Nihon}} and {{jalink|younger brother Tachibanahime no Mikoto}}. According to the shrine's legend, Nihon Takeru rested on a mountain road in this area during his {{jalink|Nihon Takeru#Eastern Conquest||Eastern Expedition}}, and the historic site was called ``Azumaya-san Gongen'' and was built to enshrine him. There is also a theory that it is compared to ``{{jalink|Kaina Shrine|Q11577877}}'' of Shikinai Shrine (``Shaki''), and there is also a theory that it is compared to ``{{jalink|Kaina Shrine|Q11577877}}''. . {{jalink|Tenmei|Q1194121|Tenmei years}} (1781 - "{{jalink|Koyo Essay}}" written by {{jalink|Tosei Kagami}} in 1789) records that it was once {{jalink|Sosha}} of Yamanashi District, and in {{jalink|Kaei|Q1146125}}4 ({{jalink|1851|Q7656}}), "{{jalink|Kaei|Q1146125}}" {jalink|Kai Sōki}}'' claims that {{jalink|Imperial envoy|Q11399920}} was sent when a major disaster or disaster occurred in Kai Province's {{jalink|Soja|Q1107129}}, but even if this is true, it seems that it was made a sessha of the head office at a fairly early stage.. Ieyasu's {{jalink|shuinjo|Q11093673}} dated 11th year of Tensho states that in addition to the head office, there were various towns such as Kokuga, Shizume, and Manriki.For Kokuga, see the note above, and Manriki is present-day Manriki, Yamanashi City. 1 kan 300 mon is relieved as ``{{jalink|Kai Province|Q858076|Koshu}} Shiasan territory.'' {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}8 ({{jalink|1603|Q6735}}) {{jalink|Four Tokugawa Magistrates|Q11490263}}{{jalink|Kuroinjo|Q 11678035}} also appears to be ``Shiazan Gongen,'' and a little more than 1 koku of divine land was donated ("Shaki"), but in September of the first year of the Keian period, more than 1 koku was made into a vermilion stamp land and was granted exemption from various duties such as forestry, bamboo, and wood. It was completely destroyed by fire in {{jalink|1989|Q2425}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} first year), but was rebuilt in {{jalink|1992|Q2060}} (1992). Furthermore, the {{jalink|cedar}} {{jalink|sacred tree|Q48743901}}, which had been designated as a {{jalink|natural monument|Q23790}} in Kasugai Town as a botanically valuable tree, was also destroyed in the fire (the tree was estimated to be 350 years old at the time of the burn).There are also two other shrines within the precincts of the head office: Tennogu Shrine and Shigeo Inari Shrine. === Old {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} | Founding = (tradition) {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}} morning |Main shrine = Sumikiri {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781}} pine roof |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=April 4th|Q2508|WD=Q2508}}・{{ill|lt=October 15th|Q2919|WD=Q2919}} |Shinto ritual = Daidai Kagura (April 4th/{{ill|lt=April 5th|Q2507|WD=Q2507|lt=5th}})|Map = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Yamanashioka Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yamanashioka Shrine'' (Yamanashioka Shrine) is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Shizume, Kasugai-cho, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720}}{{ill|lt=Fuefuki City|Q849434|WD=Q849434}}. {{jalink|Yamanashioka Shrine}, which is compared to "Yamanashioka Shrine" in {{ill||lt=Shikiuchi|UNKNOWN}}. (Yamanashi City)|Q11469626|Yamanashioka Shrine}} also claims to be a Shikinai shrine. . Located on the north edge of the {{ill|lt=Kofu Basin|Q11577819|WD=Q11577819}}, on the right bank of the Byodo River, a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Fuefuki River|Q11599751|WD=Q11599751}}, the shrine grounds are {{jal ink|Ozokyoji Mountain|Q11438742}} It is located at the southeast foot of Mt. === Company name === Since early modern times, it has been called ``Nikko Gongen'', ``Yamanashi Gongen'', and ``Yamanashi Myojin'', but according to the ``Shrine Detail Book'' cited in the ``Investigation Report'', it began to be called ``Nikko Gongen'' in accordance with the ancient place name in the first year of the Keian era, by order of {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481|WD=Q314481}}. , in the first year of the {{ill|lt=Meiji period|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717}}), the company was renamed to its current name, ``Yamanashioka Shrine.'' === Enshrined God === {{ill|lt=Enshrined Deities|Q11591100|WD=Q11591100}} Three {{ill|lt=Hashira|Q4817|WD=Q4817}} are enshrined: {{ill||lt=Oyamazumi-kami|UNKNOWN}} (Oyamazumi-kami), {{ill|lt=Yakami-kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaoka-kami}} (Takaoka-kami-kami), and ``Wakeikazuchi-kami'' (Wakeikazuchinokami). According to the shrine's legend, three gods were enshrined from the beginning, but according to the ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}'' only one was designated as the subject of national rituals. === History === [[File:Yamanashi-oka_shrine_Koriishi.jpg|thumb|170px|Koriishi (Photographed in June 2010)]] [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №2.jpg|thumb|170px|Kaguraden (Photographed in June 2010)]] It is thought that the origin of the mountain dates back to an old belief in Mt. Omuro as a divine object, and it is speculated that the mountain name ``Mt. Omuro'' means ``a mountain where divine spirits are imprisoned,'' similar to ``Momoroyama'' ({{ill|lt=Mt. Miwa|Q860627|WD=Q860627}} in {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495}}. Furthermore, in ``{{ill|lt=Bunka (era name)|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka}}'' 11th year ({{ill|lt=1814|Q6943|WD=Q6943}}) in ``{{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q11577871|WD=Q11577871}}'' (hereinafter abbreviated as ``Kokushi''), there is a mention that Mt. Omuro once rang as a sign of abnormalities in the country.According to the shrine's legend, in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, due to an epidemic of epidemics, three deities were enshrined on the peak of Mt. Nikko Takachiho (which is believed to be Mt. Omuro behind this shrine) by imperial order and served as the guardian of the nearby area. Q316672}}, when establishing the district boundaries, several {{ill|lt=pear||WD=|lt=pear trees}} at the current shrine site at the foot of the mountain were cut down and relocated, and the enshrinement site was named ``Kaigane Yamanashioka.'' ``Shrine Detail Book'' cited in ``Investigation Report.'' "Japanese Gods". . Even today, there is a ``Koriishi'' in the precincts that is said to have been a marker stone for the county border, but the place of Shizumu is {{jalink|Kai Province|Q85807 of {{ill|lt=Reisei Province|Q860290|WD=Q860290|lt=Reisei Province}} 6}}{{ill|lt=Yamanashi District|Q11469930|WD=Q11469930}}{{ill||lt=Yamanashi-go|UNKNOWN}}, so according to this shrine tradition, it is the shrine where the name of the county at the time and the current name of the prefecture originated.The Kasugai area, located in the Yamanashi district of ancient Kai Province, is home to {{ill||lt=Late Kofun|UNKNOWN}} and {{jalink|immigrants} from the late {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|WD=Q459939}} to ancient times. |Q11561926}} Archaeological ruins such as {{ill|lt=Stacked stone mound|Q11597152|WD=Q11597152}}, which is thought to be a tomb system, are distributed in Kai 4 districts (Yamanashi, {{ill|lt=Yashiro districts After the establishment of (Kai Province|Q11390569|WD=Q11390569|lt=Yashiro}}, {{ill|lt=Koma-gun||WD=|lt=Koma}}, {{ill|lt=Tsuru-gun|Q11643756|WD=Q11643756}}), the early Kokufu of Kai and the {{jalin of Yamanashi District] It is thought that it was the location of the k|gunge|Q108779163}}, and there are collections of {{ill|lt=Nara period|Q189178|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara}} and {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} {{ill|lt=Teramoto Abandoned Temple|Q11457899|WD=Q11457899}}, which is thought to be a fallen ruin or an ancient {{ill|lt=family temple|Q11547815|WD=Q11547815}} of {{ill|lt=powerful family|Q84066|WD=Q84066}}. There are also {{ill|lt=Kanga|Q104539349|WD=Q104539349|lt=Government facilities}} such as ``Yamanashi Prefectural History'', Comprehensive History Volume 1, Primitive and Antiquity (2003), etc. for information on the aspects of ancient Kai Province and Yamanashi County. . In addition, there are 20 Shikinai shrines in Kai Province listed in the "Engishiki Jinmyocho", but about half of them, 9, were located in Yamanashi District. (Koshu City)||Kinbe Shrine}} and Yamanashi City's {{ill|lt=Kinbe Shrine (Yamanashi City)||WD=|lt=Same name}} are Ronsha) and {{ill||lt=Monobe Shrine|UNKNOWN}} There are similar shrines in other countries such as "(Fuefuki City) || Mononobe Shrine}}" (Matsumoto Chinza, Isawa-cho, Fuefuki City), but this shrine is located within the ceremony "{{ill|lt=Kaina Shrine|Q11577877|WD=Q11577877}}" (There are multiple Ronshas, and our shrine's set shrine, Azuma-ya-miya, is one of them. Along with other shrines such as ``Oimata Shrine'' (which also has multiple Ronsha shrines, such as {{ill||lt=Kubohachiman Shrine|UNKNOWN}} in Kita Chinza, Yamanashi City), it is a shrine unique to Kai Province that bears the name of a place in Kai Province (Masato Hara, ``Divinity Award and Local Society,'' Prefectural History Volume 1, Chapter 6, Section 3). . Furthermore, the place name ``Kou'' (Kofu, Kasugai-cho, Fuefuki City) that remains to the east of the enshrined place is considered to be the location of the early {{ill|lt=Kokufu|Q1629088|WD=Q1629088}} of Kai Province, and is considered to be the ``Gods of Japan''. , {{ill||lt=Noin Hoshi|UNKNOWN}} wrote a poem saying, ``Yamanashinohana of Yamanashi-no-oka, which blooms on the Kai ridge and drags its feet'' (``{{ill||lt=Fukishu|UNKNOWN}}''), and it is believed that this is because the shrine was famous since ancient times due to its proximity to the Kokufu.``Investigation report''. .According to the company biography, {{ill|lt=Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=In the Sengoku period}}, the leader of the {{ill|lt=Kai Genji|Q11577904|WD=Q11577904}} {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|WD=Q1210114}} It is said that they sent messengers to visit the shrine every September, and it was compiled in {{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|WD=Q776775}}4 ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717}}). "{{ill||lt=Kaikoku Shrine and Temple Records|UNKNOWN}}From July 4th year of Keio to 1st year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=Egen changed to|Q11498853|WD=Q11498853}} in September 4th Keio)" This is a compilation of the history and traditions of shrines and {{ill|lt=temples|Q5393308|WD=Q5393308}} in Yamanashi Prefecture that were submitted to the head of the Kofuji Shrine. '' (hereinafter referred to as the ``Shaki'') also states that whenever the Takeda army went to battle, a shrine visit was held as the {{ill|lt=guardian deity|Q767655|WD=Q767655}} of {{ill|lt=Tsujigasaki-kan|Q11637200|WD=Q11637200}}, and various offerings were made. However, at the end of the Sengoku period, around the beginning of the {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tensho era}} (later 16th century), the clan declined, and over the shrine position, Hashidate Myojin Shrine (currently Hashidate Chinza, Ichinomiya-cho, Fuefuki City, {{ill|lt=Kaina Shrine #Kaina Shrine (Hashidate, Ichinomiya-cho, Fuefuki City)|Q11577877|WD=Q11577877|lt=Kaina Shrine}}) and {{ill||lt=dispute|UNKNOWN}} arose, and the Negi named Fujibei of this shrine was expelled from his position by the {{ill||lt=Negi|UNKNOWN}} of the Hashidate Myojin Shrine, and was placed under the umbrella of the same shrine. At that time, Hashidate Myojin had a powerful position that boasted its status as the ``parent deity of Kai Province'' (Kokushi), with shrine members from shrines east of the Fuefuki River gathering for its festivals (Kokushi), and it is thought that the shrine was intended to be under its control based on that authority; , after the {{ill|lt=Honnoji Incident|Q169598|WD=Q169598}} in the 10th year of Tensho ({{ill|lt=1582|Q6719|WD=Q6719}}), Kai Province fought between {{ill|lt=Ujinao Hojo|Q1156668|WD=Q1156668}} and {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977}}. When it came to the place of capture, the Hashidate Myojin {{ill|lt=shake|Q11588367|WD=Q11588367|lt=shake shu}} sided with the Hojo side, and when the Hojo army was defeated by the Tokugawa army, the shrine shu were also annihilated and their authority was lost, and in October of the same year, the Tokugawa clan returned the position of Negi-shoku to Tobei, breaking away from Hashidate Myojin's control. According to the "Shaki", various towns such as Shizume, Komatsu, Yamazaki, and Kokuga, which were designated as {{ill||lt=jinryo|UNKNOWN}} by Ieyasu on April 18, 1988, are also located in the Kasugai area (Komatsu, Kasugai-cho, Fuefuki City), Yamazaki is in the Isawa area (Yamazaki, Isawa-cho, same city), and Kokuga is in the Misaka area (Kokuga, Misaka-machi, same city). Total 4{{ill|lt=kan|Q116911247|WD=Q116911247}}803{{ill|lt=text (Currency unit)|Q6279611|WD=Q6279611|lt=Text}} was {{ill|lt=relief|Q11450498|WD=Q11450498}}, and at the same time, the divine territory of Azumaya-san Gongen (Azumaya Shrine), which was {{ill|lt=Setsusha|Q135009904|WD=Q135009904}}, was also relieved as ``Shiasan territory.'' These were also confirmed and maintained in {{ill|lt=Ina Tadatsugu|Q3387913|WD=Q3387913}}'s five country sou {{ill|lt=chiken|Q1092846|WD=Q1092846}} in the same year, but Tadatsugu also relieved {{ill||lt=Kuroinji|UNKNOWN}} on November 23 of the same year (``Kokushi'').In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, in August of the first year of {{ill|lt=Keian|Q1146135|WD=Q1146135}} ({{ill|lt=1648|Q6941|WD=Q6941}}), 8{{jalink|stone (Unit) | Q855048| Mountains were also exempted from various duties such as red stamp land and forestry bamboo trees, but these red seal land were followed by successive {{ill|lt=Seii Taishogun|Q131767|WD=Q131767|lt=shogun}} thereafter. In the 5th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), it was listed as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Shinto ritual === [[File:Yamananashi-oka shrine Daidai Kagura A.jpg|thumb|170px|Ama no Iwato no Mai from Daidai Kagura (Photographed on April 4, 2011)]] [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine Daidai Kagura B.JPG|left|thumb|170px|Ama no Iwato Dance from Daidai Kagura (Photographed April 4, 2011)]] There are festivals held in spring and autumn, and according to the "Shaki", in the past, {{ill|lt=portable shrines|Q979940|WD=Q979940}} were carried to Mt. Omuro, the former site of the shrine, on {{ill|lt=Hatsuuma|Q510847|WD=Q510847}} days in March and November. Currently, the annual festival is held on {{ill|lt=April 4th|Q2508|WD=Q2508}} and {{ill|lt=October 15th|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}.At the annual spring festival, {{ill||lt=Daida Kagura|UNKNOWN}} is performed for two days and nights (April 4th and 5th). It is also called ``Takeda Shingen's Departure Kagura,'' and according to the shrine's legend, it was dedicated to the Takeda army as a prayer for victory when they departed for battle. The dancers are called "Maiko" and were once performed by {{ill|lt=Shinto priests|Q60523|WD=Q60523}}, but later they were selected from among the {{ill|lt=parishioners|Q125562175|WD=Q125562175|lt=middle parishioners}} and have been passed down to the present day.{{j alink|hayashi|Q4171186}} is played by three types of instruments: {{ill|lt=taiko|Q11404|WD=Q11404}}, {{ill|lt=kengu|Q15614634|WD=Q15614634}}, and {{ill|lt=flute|Q11405|WD=Q11405}}. Furthermore, the ``Shaki'' records the origin of this kagura in {{ill|lt=Enpo|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1675|Q7624|WD=Q7624}}), which is considered to be the time when offerings at spring festivals became established.``Encyclopedia of Japanese Festival Culture.'' . {{ill|lt=1967|Q2648|WD=Q2648}} ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}}42) On August 7, it was designated as an {{ill||lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Property|UNKNOWN}} of Yamanashi Prefecture.It belongs to the so-called {{ill||lt=Izumo Kagura|UNKNOWN}} lineage, which expresses the world of {{ill|lt=Japanese mythology|Q814158|WD=Q814158|lt=Kikiki mythology}}, centering on the story of {{ill||lt=Amano Iwato|UNKNOWN}}. There are 24 types of dances that have been passed down, and the order of number 24 is: 1. Four directions dance, 2. Funeral dance, 3. Gokansho dance, 4. Kunijotachi no Mikoto dance, 5. Yin-Yang dance, 6. Ukebochi no dance, 7. Four bow dance, 8. Dance of Tenden-onna no Mikoto, 9. Dance of Life of Sarutahiko, 10. Dance of Orochi, 11. Dance of Ama-no-Iwato, 12. Dance of Offerings, 13. Dance of Oratory, 14. Dance of Two Bows, 15. Dance of Kotosushiro no Mikoto, 16. Dance of Two Swords, 17. Five elements dance, 18. Dongyoku dance, 19. Naginata Ryoken dance, 20. Four swords dance (Kume mai), 21. Houken Kyozo dance, 22. Chihiki (Chihiki) dance, 23. Bow tengu dance, 24. Oyama Gimei dance. It is said that the program and structure (sequence) of the songs have not changed since ancient times, starting with dances to purify the place where kagura is dedicated, such as ``Shiho-mai'' and ``Female Hall Dance.'' At {{ill|lt=noon|Q168182|WD=Q168182}}, the centerpiece ``Ama no Iwato-no-Mai'' is dedicated, and finally, ``Oyama-Gi-no-Mikoto-no-Mai,'' in which the deity Oyama-Gumi appears, is supposed to be performed. Maiko is basically a male, but only the 8th song "Tencho Onna no Mikoto no Mai" and the 19th song "Naginata Ryoken no Mai" are girls with {{ill|lt=Facing|Q119379771|WD=Q119379771|lt=Facing (Hitamen)}} The dance and the final song, ``Oyamazumi no Mikoto'', is performed by the deity enshrined at this shrine (Oyamazumi no Kami), so it is decided that the chief maiko (Maikocho, the head of the maiko) will perform the dance. In addition, the 20th song, ``Four Swords Dance,'' is performed by four people using swords and bells as {{ill|lt=Forest|Q11498259|WD=Q11498259}}, but it is also called ``Kume Mai'' because it is named after {{ill|lt=Kume Mai|Q55529628|WD=Q55529628}}. === Kami no Kami === [[File:KINOKAMI Legendary creatures.JPG|left|thumb|100px|True image of KINOKAMI KINOKAMI. {{ill|lt=Kyowa|Q960763|WD=Q960763}}You can see the description of Spring New Year of the second year. (Photo taken on April 4, 2011)]] [[File:Shan Hai Jing Kui.jpg|right|thumb|140px|{{ill|lt=Shanhai Ching|Q844278|WD=Q844278}} "夔(Ki)"]] [[File:Ogyuh Sorai.jpg|250px|right|thumb|Ogyuh Sorai]] In front of the main shrine of Yamanashioka Shrine, a {{ill|lt=statue|Q11588751|WD=Q11588751}} called ``Ki no Kami'' is enshrined. Cultural property (sculpture) designated by Fuefuki City. According to the 2nd year of the Keio era, ``Kinokami Raiyuki'' ({{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefectural Museum|Q11469696|WD=Q11469696}} collection), the age and provenance of the statue are unknown, but it is assumed that it was before {{ill|lt=Hoei|Q1145262|WD=Q1145262}}3 years ({{ill|lt=1706|Q6835|WD=Q6835}}) based on the age of the recorded materials (described later).It is said to have been created by a craftsman from Hida, , and is approximately 66 cm tall. It is a strange statue of a lone-legged beast with {{ill|lt=tusks|Q185635|WD=Q185635}} and {{ill||lt=mane|UNKNOWN}} called ``{{ill|lt=夔|Q2987501|WD=Q2987501}}'' that appears in the ancient Chinese geography book ``{{ill|lt=Shanhaijing|Q844278|WD=Q844278}}'' and is believed to be a talisman and ward off lightning. Although it is not normally possible to worship, the gates are opened once every 10 years, and now once every seven years.For more information on the Kōkami statue and the Kōkami belief, see Yoriko Maruo, ``God? Beast? The Descent of the Gods'' (Yamanashi Prefectural Museum, 2009). In the literature, it can be found in {{ill||lt=Kyōchu Kiko|UNKNOWN}} by Sorai Ogyu in the 3rd year of the Hoei era ({{ill|lt=1706|Q6835|WD=Q6835}}). Ogyu Sorai was a Confucian scholar who was a vassal of the {{ill|lt=Kofu Domain|Q661116|WD=Q661116}} lord and {{ill|lt=Yanagisawa Yoshiyasu|Q846911|WD=Q846911}}.He traveled around Koshu on Yoshiyasu's orders, and when he visited Yamanashioka Shrine, even the priest did not know the origin of the statue. In addition, in 1886 (Q7822}}, a pastor and folk researcher who stayed in Kofu, "Kai It is also mentioned in ``The Fallen Leaves'', and Kyōko speculates that the statue, which was originally a {{ill|lt=guardian dog|Q20155846|WD=Q20155846}}, was associated with the god of war and was worshiped as a protection against thunder.According to "Rayuki", in the {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|lt=Kansei period}} (end of the 18th century), Teiichiro Yasuda, the accountant of the shogunate, visited this shrine on the occasion of river inspection. |Kyowa|Q960763}} In the 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1802|Q6883|WD=Q6883}}), the {{ill||lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|UNKNOWN}} ordered him to present the True Image of the Goddess. At this time, several hundred true images of the gods were taken and presented to {{ill|lt=Ooku|Q1064983|WD=Q1064983}}, {{ill|lt=Gosanke|Q11071135|WD=Q11071135}}, {{ill|lt=Hatamoto|Q572662|WD=Q572662}}, and others. Furthermore, in the late Edo period, interest in {{ill|lt=Yōkai|Q503186|WD=Q503186}} increased due to social unrest such as disasters and famine, and it is said that the belief in Kokugami spread among the general public. In addition, books of history have been disseminated, stories of miraculous experiences have also spread, and anecdotes have spread such as how the Takeda family brought disaster to the Oda army upon the downfall of the Takeda clan. It is estimated that the peak of the Kugami belief was around the Kyōwa period mentioned above, based on the state of wear of the Shinkage woodblocks, and in modern times hanging scrolls from the {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō period}} can be seen, and divine cards are still being distributed today.Yamanashioka Shrine is a shrine that enshrines nature gods such as the {{ill|lt=Mountain God|Q839254|WD=Q839254}}, the god of water and rain, and the god of thunder, and it has been pointed out that the existence of folk beliefs in nature is behind the spread of the belief in the kami. In Yamanashi Prefecture, there is widespread belief in mountain gods, and {{ill|lt=rain praying|Q477738|WD=Q477738}} customs and {{ill||lt=thunder worship|UNKNOWN}} are also seen. Furthermore, in the {{ill|lt=Dosojin|Q269515|WD=Q269515}} faith that is common in Yamanashi Prefecture, there is a custom of decorating large grass sandals as part of the {{ill|lt=Michikiri|Q11641628|WD=Q11641628}} event, so it is thought that the one-legged figure of the mountain god was commonly thought of. === Shrine building === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №3.jpg|thumb|170px|Main shrine (photographed in June 2010)]] [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №4.jpg|thumb|170px|Inside the shrine (taken in June 2010)]]{{ill|lt=Main shrine|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994}} is said to be the work of {{ill||lt=Takumi of Hida|UNKNOWN}}. It is also said to have been built by a bansho|Q11578735}}, and although the details are unknown, it is estimated that it was built at the end of the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845}} based on its structural style. The roof is {{ill|lt=杮thatched|Q199388|WD=Q199388}}, and it is a one-ken shrine {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781}} with 1 front beam and 2 back beams, and 2 girders. It is a ``Sumiki-iri Kasuga-zukuri'' style in which a {{ill||lt=sumiki|UNKNOWN}} is placed on the pillar in front of the {{ill||lt=personal building|UNKNOWN}} (moya), and a {{ill|lt=kohai|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970}} (kohai) is attached to the front. It is large in scale, and the characteristics of the time when it was built, aiming for new expressions, can be recognized in the overall variety of techniques and detailed designs such as the carving of a picture of a frog. . {{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}40) On August 28th, special It was designated as a protected building and was enacted in {{ill|lt=Cultural Properties Protection Law|Q1188502|WD=Q1188502}} in {{ill|lt=1950|Q18597|WD=Q18597}} (Showa 25)It is designated as a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}. Furthermore, in the article dated September 7th and 8th of the first year of the {{ill|lt=Kyoroku|Q1193418|WD=Q1193418}} year ({{ill|lt=1528|Q6415|WD=Q6415}}) of ``{{ill||lt=Odaiki|UNKNOWN}}'', there is a divine miracle that blood flowed from the {{ill|lt=rafter|Q1154705|WD=Q1154705}}, but its relationship to the current main shrine is unknown.The {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745}} is {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025}} with 5 rows and 4 beams, {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025}} flat entrance. The roof is {{ill||lt=thatched roof|UNKNOWN}} (originally {{ill|lt=thatched roof|Q874531|WD=Q874531}}). Constructed in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}}15 ({{ill|lt=1702|Q6819|WD=Q6819}}), it has an atmosphere reminiscent of a Buddhist temple, and although it has not been designated as such, it is considered to be a valuable relic for understanding the prefectural early modern architectural style. Others include {{ill|lt=Kaguraden|Q94498|WD=Q94498}}. === Setsusha === ;Setsusha Azumaya Shrine:It is located halfway up Kitayama, about 1km north of Yamanashioka Shrine (hereinafter referred to as the headquarters). , enshrines {{ill||lt=Takeru Nihon|UNKNOWN}} and {{ill||lt=younger brother Tachibanahime no Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}. According to the shrine's legend, Nihon Takeru rested on a mountain road in this area during his {{ill|lt=Nihon Takeru#Eastern Conquest||WD=|lt=Eastern Expedition}}, and the historic site was called ``Azumaya-san Gongen'' and was built to enshrine him. There is also a theory that it is compared to ``{{ill|lt=Kaina Shrine|Q11577877|WD=Q11577877}}'' of Shikinai Shrine (``Shaki''), and there is also a theory that it is compared to ``{{ill|lt=Kaina Shrine|Q11577877|WD=Q11577877}}''. . {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|WD=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei years}} (1781 - "{{ill||lt=Koyo Essay|UNKNOWN}}" written by {{ill||lt=Tosei Kagami|UNKNOWN}} in 1789) records that it was once {{ill||lt=Sosha|UNKNOWN}} of Yamanashi District, and in {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125}}4 ({{ill|lt=1851|Q7656|WD=Q7656}}), "{{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125}}" {jalink|Kai Sōki}}'' claims that {{ill|lt=Imperial envoy|Q11399920|WD=Q11399920}} was sent when a major disaster or disaster occurred in Kai Province's {{ill|lt=Soja|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129}}, but even if this is true, it seems that it was made a sessha of the head office at a fairly early stage.. Ieyasu's {{ill|lt=shuinjo|Q11093673|WD=Q11093673}} dated 11th year of Tensho states that in addition to the head office, there were various towns such as Kokuga, Shizume, and Manriki.For Kokuga, see the note above, and Manriki is present-day Manriki, Yamanashi City. 1 kan 300 mon is relieved as ``{{ill|lt=Kai Province|Q858076|WD=Q858076|lt=Koshu}} Shiasan territory.'' {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}8 ({{ill|lt=1603|Q6735|WD=Q6735}}) {{ill|lt=Four Tokugawa Magistrates|Q11490263|WD=Q11490263}}{{ill||lt=Kuroinjo|UNKNOWN}} also appears to be ``Shiazan Gongen,'' and a little more than 1 koku of divine land was donated ("Shaki"), but in September of the first year of the Keian period, more than 1 koku was made into a vermilion stamp land and was granted exemption from various duties such as forestry, bamboo, and wood. It was completely destroyed by fire in {{ill|lt=1989|Q2425|WD=Q2425}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} first year), but was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1992|Q2060|WD=Q2060}} (1992). Furthermore, the {{ill||lt=cedar|UNKNOWN}} {{ill|lt=sacred tree|Q48743901|WD=Q48743901}}, which had been designated as a {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790}} in Kasugai Town as a botanically valuable tree, was also destroyed in the fire (the tree was estimated to be 350 years old at the time of the burn).There are also two other shrines within the precincts of the head office: Tennogu Shrine and Shigeo Inari Shrine. === Cultural assets === [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine Wisteria.JPG| thumb|260px|Fuji, a city natural monument (taken on May 6, 2011)]] === Important cultural property Old {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} | Founding = (tradition) {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}} morning |Main shrine = Sumikiri {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781}} pine roof |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=April 4th|Q2508|WD=Q2508}}・{{ill|lt=October 15th|Q2919|WD=Q2919}} |Shinto ritual = Daidai Kagura (April 4th/{{ill|lt=April 5th|Q2507|WD=Q2507|lt=5th}})|Map = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Yamanashioka Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Yamanashioka Shrine'' (Yamanashioka Shrine) is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Shizume, Kasugai-cho, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|WD=Q132720}}{{ill|lt=Fuefuki City|Q849434|WD=Q849434}}. {{jalink|Yamanashioka Shrine}, which is compared to "Yamanashioka Shrine" in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikiuchi|UNKNOWN}}. (Yamanashi City)|Q11469626|Yamanashioka Shrine}} also claims to be a Shikinai shrine. . Located on the north edge of the {{ill|lt=Kofu Basin|Q11577819|WD=Q11577819}}, on the right bank of the Byodo River, a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Fuefuki River|Q11599751|WD=Q11599751}}, the shrine grounds are {{jal ink|Ozokyoji Mountain|Q11438742}} It is located at the southeast foot of Mt. === Company name === Since early modern times, it has been called ``Nikko Gongen'', ``Yamanashi Gongen'', and ``Yamanashi Myojin'', but according to the ``Shrine Detail Book'' cited in the ``Investigation Report'', it began to be called ``Nikko Gongen'' in accordance with the ancient place name in the first year of the Keian era, by order of {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|Q314481|WD=Q314481}}. , in the first year of the {{ill|lt=Meiji period|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} ({{ill|lt=1868|Q7717|WD=Q7717}}), the company was renamed to its current name, ``Yamanashioka Shrine.'' === Enshrined God === {{ill|lt=Enshrined Deities|Q11591100|WD=Q11591100}} Three {{ill|lt=Hashira|Q4817|WD=Q4817}} are enshrined: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Oyamazumi-kami|UNKNOWN}} (Oyamazumi-kami), {{ill|lt=Yakami-kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaoka-kami}} (Takaoka-kami-kami), and ``Wakeikazuchi-kami'' (Wakeikazuchinokami). According to the shrine's legend, three gods were enshrined from the beginning, but according to the ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}'' only one was designated as the subject of national rituals. === History === [[File:Yamanashi-oka_shrine_Koriishi.jpg|thumb|170px|Koriishi (Photographed in June 2010)]] [[File:Yamanashi-oka shrine №2.jpg|thumb|170px|Kaguraden (Photographed in June 2010)]] It is thought that the origin of the mountain dates back to an old belief in Mt. Omuro as a divine object, and it is speculated that the mountain name ``Mt. Omuro'' means ``a mountain where divine spirits are imprisoned,'' similar to ``Momoroyama'' ({{ill|lt=Mt. Miwa|Q860627|WD=Q860627}} in {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495}}. Furthermore, in ``{{ill|lt=Bunka (era name)|Q1373146|WD=Q1373146|lt=Bunka}}'' 11th year ({{ill|lt=1814|Q6943|WD=Q6943}}) in ``{{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q11577871|WD=Q11577871}}'' (hereinafter abbreviated as ``Kokushi''), there is a mention that Mt. Omuro once rang as a sign of abnormalities in the country.According to the shrine's legend, in the era of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, due to an epidemic of epidemics, three deities were enshrined on the peak of Mt. Nikko Takachiho (which is believed to be Mt. Omuro behind this shrine) by imperial order and served as the guardian of the nearby area. Q316672}}, when establishing the district boundaries, several {{ill|lt=pear||WD=|lt=pear trees}} at the current shrine site at the foot of the mountain were cut down and relocated, and the enshrinement site was named ``Kaigane Yamanashioka.'' ``Shrine Detail Book'' cited in ``Investigation Report.''
